title: Cat Bumping into Objects: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat bumping into walls or furniture? Discover the common causes of feline vision loss and when to contact your veterinarian immediately.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat begins bumping into furniture, walls, or stationary objects, it is usually a clinical sign of sudden vision impairment or a neurological coordination issue. This behavior indicates that your cat is having difficulty perceiving their environment or navigating spatial boundaries. Common causes include feline hypertension, cataracts, retinal detachment, or vestibular disease.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. While this situation is deeply concerning, it is typically classified as a medium-level urgency rather than an immediate life-threatening emergency, provided your cat is still breathing normally and conscious. However, sudden onset blindness can be a symptom of an underlying medical crisis, such as extremely high blood pressure. You should contact a veterinarian for an appointment within the next 12 to 24 hours to prevent permanent damage or further complications.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Taking a clear, close-up photo of your cat’s eyes in a well-lit room can provide vital clues to a triage team. They will look for dilated pupils that do not shrink with light, cloudiness, or redness. Furthermore, a short video of your cat walking can help the vet distinguish between a visual problem (bumping into things) and a balance problem (staggering or leaning), which helps determine the severity of the case.

faqs.question: Could my cat have high blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Yes, feline hypertension is a common cause of sudden blindness, especially in senior cats, and often leads to retinal detachment if left untreated.

faqs.question: Is sudden blindness in cats permanent?
faqs.answer: Not always. If the underlying cause, such as high blood pressure or certain inflammations, is treated quickly, some cats can regain part or all of their vision.

faqs.question: Should I move my furniture if my cat can't see?
faqs.answer: It is best to keep your home layout exactly the same. Cats rely on scent and memory to navigate; moving furniture can cause more confusion and potential injury.
